data.name: Brice Samba
data.description: Samba was once the back-up to Steve Mandanda at club level, but partnering him in the France national team – for a long time – seemed improbable. After Marseille, Samba had stints at Nancy, Caen and Nottingham Forest, helping the latter to earn promotion back to the Premier League in his final season at the club. Promotion was a parting gift, with Samba crucial in a penalty shootout in the playoffs. "My journey is a bit mad," he admits. "It may seem mad, but I always believed [in earning a France call-up]." Down in the Championship, it certainly seemed a long way away, and even more surprising was his decision to leave Forest, just at the point that they had returned to the top flight. The decision paid off though; one year later, he was playing in the Champions League with Lens and had made the France team.

data.name: Malo Gusto
data.description: The defender recently outlined his big ambitions. "My goal is to be the best in the world in my position," he said in March. To do that, there is work to be done. "I'm not with Les Bleus to sit on the bench my whole life," he has also said. He is currently not first-choice for club nor country, even if Reece James's ability to play in midfield has afforded him more minutes for Chelsea this season. Gusto is driven to succeed in the sport, even if he has vocalised his misgivings about the football world more generally. "Honestly, I don't like it at all. At the start, it was a dream, a parallel world, but now I'm in it, I see the back side; it doesn't necessarily align with my values." He says he plays to make his family proud, and he will look to do that for France this summer.

data.name: Lucas Digne
data.description: For a player who has represented clubs of the calibre of Lille, Roma, Paris Saint-Germain and Barcelona, you wouldn't expect that the majority of his appearances for France would have come while playing for Everton and Aston Villa. While at some of Europe's elite clubs, the left-back role was never truly his own, but it is now. “I feel as though I’m playing the best football of my career,” he says. “I am a happy footballer, a happy man, and a happy father. I’m loving what I’m doing.” Digne attributes his current status to the Aston Villa manager, Unai Emery. "He took me to the next level," says Digne. For the full-back, it was initially going down a level that has allowed him to make that step up.

data.name: Dayot Upamecano
data.description: Football has given Upamecano a voice that he had struggled to find. "When I was at school, I had a lot of difficulties … I had a stammer. Some of my schoolmates made fun of me," the Bayern Munich defender once said. "But on the pitch, I have always been [vocal]. People said: 'Dayot, he doesn't speak a lot, but when he's playing football, he shouts!'" He added: "People have often told me that I'm not the same on the pitch as I am off it. In life, I am quite calm and reserved but when I get on the pitch, I am like a lion coming out of a cage!" He has been doing more than just fine, growing as a presence for club and country and asserting himself as a first-choice option for both. Joined Bayern Munich in 2022 from RB Leipzig.

data.name: Jules Koundé
data.description: Didier Deschamps has previously referred to France's problem of producing full-backs, and within that context, Koundé has emerged as a solution, albeit not a willing one initially. "I learned to accept it because, subconsciously, I think you create blockages by being negative, and I must admit that I have in the past," says the Barcelona defender, a centre-back by trade but who now plays on the right for club and country. His discomfort in the position relates to the attacking demands. He is not the most eye-catching in that sense, but he certainly catches the eye off the pitch thanks to his bold fashion choices. "It is a part of my personality; I like to dress well," says Koundé, who has made the right-back slot his own at international level.

data.name: Manu Koné
data.description: Koné's trajectory isn't an unfamiliar one in French football. He left his formative club, Toulouse, when he was still just a teenager, to join Borussia Mönchengladbach. His performances there earned him a move to Roma where he has truly flourished. Pretty much all of his development has happened outside of the French gaze. "I worked so hard, under the radar, you could say," he said in September. Reaching the France team is one thing, but heading into the World Cup, he is very much a contender for a starting spot. Didier Deschamps is a big fan of Koné, describing him as "a complete, modern midfielder". The France manager added that the Roma midfielder is underrated. Play a prominent role in the World Cup and he will be thrust out of the shadows and into the light.

data.name: Ousmane Dembélé
data.description: In any normal team, the parts would be fitted around the Ballon d'Or winner, but that is not the case with France. Speaking alongside Didier Deschamps at an awards ceremony at the end of the 2024-25 season, Dembélé detailed why his move into the No 9 role for Paris Saint-Germain had proved so beneficial, to the extent that he was the catalyst behind Les Parisiens' maiden Champions League trophy. Dembélé knows Deschamps won't follow Luis Enrique's lead. "He still plays me on the right!" joked Dembélé. "I'll put you on the left," responded Deschamps. In any case, the central role is Mbappé's. On a serious note, getting the best out of Dembélé remains something of a headache for Deschamps. The Ballon d'Or winner's form at club level has not been replicated at international level, where he has just seven goals in 58 appearances at the time of writing.

data.name: Aurélien Tchouaméni
data.description: Big-money moves always garner the most scrutiny, but judging their success is not always easy, especially for a midfielder, whose effectiveness is not necessarily reflected in stats. But there is a telling one: nearly 200 appearances for Real Madrid in under four years. Those have come since Tchouaméni’s €100m move from Monaco in 2022. Despite the presence of Toni Kroos and Luka Modric for much of that time, to have racked up so many appearances is a testament to his importance. He is equally crucial for France; on his current trajectory, he could be on course to make 100 appearances for Les Bleus. Jules Koundé, who knows him well, having come through the academy with him at Bordeaux, describes Tchouaméni as a "discreet and calm" character and that transmits on the pitch, where his ability to screen the defence is so valuable for club and country. There was nothing calm about his confrontation with his Real teammate Fede Valverde in May, however, with the Uruguayan ending up in hospital, requiring stitches.

data.name: Marcus Thuram
data.description: It must have been a proud dad moment for Lilian Thuram who, in a viral video, was seen waving off his sons, Khéphren and Marcus, on their way to international duty. Like kids being bid farewell by a parent, the pair looked a little embarrassed as they got into the car that would take them into Clairefontaine. Marcus is a very different player to his father – and his brother too – he has gradually worked his way up the ladder to lead the attack of one of Europe's elite sides. He has a strong record for Inter, but even more important, an excellent relationship with his strike partner, Lautaro Martínez. He has yet to create that within the France national team, hence his meagre output (three goals in 33 games at the time of writing) and more limited impact. His father scored twice for Les Bleus, and while he has at least outdone Lilian on that front, he will never better his father's 142 appearances for the national team.

data.name: Kylian Mbappé
data.description: Mbappé is chasing records in North America. Starting the tournament four goals away from Miroslav Klose’s all-time World Cup record of 16, the France forward has an obsession with writing history. However, it is collective titles that drive Mbappé. After winning Ligue 1 in his first full season at Monaco, he piled up the trophies at Paris Saint-Germain before joining Real Madrid in 2024. The goals have flowed at Real to the tune of 86 in 103 games, but the major honours have not followed. There was fierce criticism over a trip to Sardinia in May while he recovered from an injury, with some Real fans accusing him of already looking ahead to the World Cup. Club issues aside, Mbappé remains essential for France. The teenager who won the World Cup in 2018 and earned the Golden Boot in the last tournament is now aiming to captain his side to victory this summer.

data.name: Michael Olise
data.description: Olise had a multitude of options but only ever had eyes for France. The Bayern Munich forward, born in London, could also have represented England, Algeria or Nigeria at international level. "I have this connection with France," said Olise following his first call-up for the senior side. Creating a connection with his France teammates was initially tough for Olise, who has had a language barrier to overcome, as became evident during his first appearance before the media. "When I think that there is potential, I persist," said Didier Deschamps. He was right to. Olise is the long-term successor to Antoine Griezmann, who was so valuable for Les Bleus for a decade before his international retirement. Olise's nonchalance, on and off the pitch, make him a difficult player to decrypt; an enigma of sorts, but an extremely talented one.

data.name: Bradley Barcola
data.description: When you play for France's biggest club, there is scrutiny. Barcola has not been spared that analysis and, at times, he has been criticised quite intensely. After some middling performances in the Champions League, one pundit called him a "little lamb". However, not everyone's opinion matters to Barcola. "I have very few friends, so when they say: 'You're doing something silly,' it's because they're right. When they tell me that I played well, it's because they're right. I don't listen to what is said outside," he says. That doesn't mean he doesn't hear it. "[My friends] are the ones who send me things that can be read in the press. Sometimes we laugh about it, but it doesn't go any further," he adds. By shutting out the noise, Barcola has become an important player for both club and country.

data.name: N'Golo Kanté
data.description: When Kanté left Chelsea off the back of a season where injuries limited him to just nine appearances in all competitions, he was written off, especially when the midfielder chose to continue his career in the Saudi Pro League. But Deschamps is loyal; his selection choices have proven that in recent years, and all the France manager needed to be convinced to reintegrate Kanté was for him to play. He did that in the Gulf state. Not only was Kanté back for Euro 2024, he even wore the captain's armband once, for the very first time. His humility has become his trademark, but it can also have the effect of masking his great ambition. "I don't simply dream of playing at the World Cup, I want to win it," said Kanté, whose days in bleu, many thought, were numbered. He arrives at the World Cup having played at Fenerbahce since the start of February.

data.name: Adrien Rabiot
data.description: It is paradoxical how a player whose career has been so volatile can be so regularly reliable. That's Rabiot: the player who refused a place in Didier Deschamps' reserve squad in 2018 and then left his formative club, Paris Saint-Germain, in acrimonious circumstances soon after. His spell at Juventus allowed him to regain his place in the France squad. He has been a mainstay at international level ever since, but drama has continued to follow him at club level. In the image of the club itself, Rabiot's time at Marseille was chaotic. There was a legal battle with PSG after an offensive banner from the capital club’s fans, targeting Rabiot’s mother, as well as fisticuffs with Jonathan Rowe last summer that brought about a swift exit. At Milan, he benefited from the guidance of Max Allegri, whom he described as a paternal figure. Calm has returned for now; it remains to be seen if Allegri’s departure in May changes that.

data.name: Ibrahima Konaté
data.description: Konaté has been cast in the “Robin” role both for club and country: always the sidekick, never the main hero. At club level, he was in the shadow of Virgil van Dijk, and in the national team, he is often partnered alongside William Saliba, similarly considered one of the best centre-backs in world football and included in the Premier League's team of the year three seasons running. It is fitting, therefore, that Konaté is a big manga fan. He said that he will often watch fight scenes to bolster his "determination" pre-match, despite derision from his Liverpool teammates. After deciding to leave Anfield this summer, Konaté may need to exude more main character energy before too long – but, now in his prime years, the Frenchman certainly has time on his hands to grow into the role. In January he had to deal with the sudden death of his father but, fittingly, scored on his return and the whole team, including Alisson, who rushed all the way from his goal, celebrated with the defender.

data.name: Mike Maignan
data.description: There was understandably a lot of scrutiny when Maignan replaced Hugo Lloris as France's No 1. When the former Tottenham goalkeeper hung up his gloves at international level, he did so as the most capped player in the nation's history. Lloris wasn't always spectacular – perhaps he never was – but he was very reliable. And so in that sense, Maignan has represented perfect continuity. The fact that he is rarely spoken about can only be viewed positively. Following in the footsteps of a legend can be daunting but Maignan has handled that, even growing into more of a leadership role inside the France dressing room. "I like to speak," he says. In 2021 he was subjected to racial abuse in a game against Juventus and wrote eloquently on Instagram: "I am not a 'victim' of racism. I'm Mike, upright, black and proud.”

data.name: William Saliba
data.description: Saliba doesn't lack self-awareness or confidence. "I am not the best defender in the world, but yeah; one of the best," says the Arsenal centre-back. If there is still progress to be made, Mikel Arteta believes Saliba will make it. "I think that he will go to a new level in the next few years," says the Arsenal manager. Getting to his current level took time and multiple loan moves back to his native France. During that time, there were questions over whether Saliba would ever make it at Arsenal. But they were no longer posed when he returned to the Emirates for the third and final time in 2022, becoming an integral part of the Arsenal defence. He now speaks of being a leader for France: the next step to becoming the best defender in the world? Those are the kinds of questions now asked.

data.name: Warren Zaïre-Emery
data.description: Zaïre-Emery was billed as France's next wonderkid, but he came back down to earth in 2025. Having become the youngest player to play for Paris Saint-Germain when he made his debut at the age of 16, he was in the France squad before he had turned 18. Scoring on debut only increased the noise. "The sky is the limit. I've never seen a player that young being so mature," said Thierry Henry, who worked with Zaïre-Emery during his time as France Under-21s manager. Fewer minutes and a loss of form saw him return to the France Under-21s last autumn. "Confidence is playing a big part," he admitted. "It is up to me to mentally go and free myself, to play how I know I can, to have that carelessness when I was young." Still only 20, he worked his way back into the PSG side this year and back into the senior side, too.

data.name: Théo Hernández
data.description: Théo, like Lucas, has followed the same career path as his father, Jean-François, who, during his playing days, featured for clubs such as Marseille and Sochaux. The Hernandez brothers are not the only siblings involved in the France national team to follow in the footsteps of their father. Marcus and Khéphren Thuram, sons of Lilian, are both regularly involved – but unlike Lilian, Jean-François is not exactly present. “He watches [his sons] alone, because he cries from virtually start to finish," Jean-François' eldest daughter, and stepsister of the Hernandez brothers, once told France Football. That was part of an investigation into Jean-François' whereabouts. He famously went missing, without any trace, back in 2004, when the pair were very young. The investigation found that Jean-François had left for Thailand before returning in 2020. He remains estranged, but will look on from afar as his two sons likely once again compete for the same left-back berth.

data.name: Désiré Doué
data.description: Off the pitch, you are given the picture of a player who is very regimented. Those who have worked with him praise his "seriousness" and "maturity" when coming through the ranks at academy level. "Everything is oriented towards performance [with Doué]," says the QPR head coach, Julien Stéphan, who worked with him at Rennes. Overall, it is an image completely at odds with the free, expressive and dazzling player that we see on a pitch, week in, week out at Paris Saint-Germain. In a world of backwards, sideways football, Doué's instinct is to provoke a one-on-one situation. He is being tipped for an even brighter future, with Stéphan saying he has the potential to be a Ballon d'Or winner. Fitting for a player whose name literally translates as “desired talented”.

data.name: Lucas Hernández
data.description: Lucas Hernandez has struggled to catch a break; injuries have always struck at the cruellest times. A starter in the team that won the World Cup in 2018, he missed almost the entirety of the 2022 edition in Qatar after rupturing an anterior cruciate ligament 13 minutes into Les Bleus' opener against Australia. He was struck with another ACL injury at the end of the 2023-24 season, ruling him out of Euro 2024. And now he is struggling to get into the Paris Saint-Germain team because it just so happens that, in Nuno Mendes, Les Parisiens have arguably the best left-back in the world, consigning Hernandez to a back-up role and naturally limiting his minutes. His status at his club is not without consequence at international level but he has still made the squad.

data.name: Jean-Philippe Mateta
data.description: For his teammates, Mateta's dream of playing for Les Bleus was not taken too seriously. “At Crystal Palace, right at the start, when I wasn’t even playing, I spoke about the France team in the dressing room and I had teammates like Wilfried Zaha who were laughing," he said. Those comments weren't meant as an attack on Zaha, even if the Ivorian did take it that way. But even a year ago, the France national team looked like a pipe dream, but one made possible by Mateta's rise to prominence in recent seasons. Fourteen goals in the 2024-25 Premier League season paved a way into the national-team setup. Given his chance, he flourished, netting two goals in his first three appearances at senior international level.

data.name: Robin Risser
data.description: Since BlueCo's arrival, Strasbourg have looked to hoover up many of the best talents across France. However, Risser, who was already within their ranks having graduated from their academy, evidently was not considered one of them. A loan in Ligue 2 with Red Star the previous season earned plaudits, but he would not make a first-team appearance for Le Racing, whose past two seasons have been devoted to developing the goalkeepers on Chelsea's books. Their loss. Risser carved out a role in a title-challenging Lens side and the level of interest that he is reportedly garnering is proof that he can reach the highest level. To do so, however, he must handle the pressure of expectation and in that regard, there is margin for improvement. His worst performance of the season – perhaps uncoincidentally – came with France's goalkeeping coach looking on from the stands.

data.name: Rayan Cherki
data.description: Cherki has always expressed his disdain for stats. It is clear where his focus lies. "Robots are good but magic is better," he recently told France Football. "It's good to complete 99% of your passes, but it's even better to produce five or six moments of genius. It's frustrating because many coaches look for players who run the most or jump the highest. For me, that's not football. My aim is to make old-style football fashionable again." Cherki, despite his best intentions, may not have the power to define where football is heading, but he does have the ability to bring us joy when we watch it and he has done that ever since breaking through at Lyon, only now he does so with greater effect and rightly earns greater plaudits. He arrives at the World Cup after a superb season at Manchester City, which included a sensational solo goal against Arsenal in the league and some showboating against the same opponents in the League Cup final. Not everyone enjoyed his keepy-uppies, though, with Ben White clattering into him moments later.

data.name: Maghnes Akliouche
data.description: Didier Deschamps quickly discovered Akliouche's character. "He is quite introverted," said the France manager. His timidness also translated on the pitch during his first stint with the France squad back in September. It was an opportunity missed for the Monaco forward, but Deschamps is one to give second chances and Akliouche seized his. As he came out of his shell off the pitch, he began to flourish on it, too, delivering the types of performances that got him into the national team setup in the first place. "France has so many players on a top level, but I cannot see a player with the same skills as Maghnes," said his former coach at Monaco, Adi Hütter. Like his competitors for a place in the squad, such as Rayan Cherki and Michael Olise, Akliouche is blessed with technical quality, but he has now started to add goals and assists to his game, too.

data.name: Maxence Lacroix
data.description: Through his words and his actions, you get the impression that Lacroix is very selfless. Through an initiative from his club, Crystal Palace, he has given out bags of clothes to homeless people in south London and he has also participated in development projects in his native village of Ajat. "I grew up watching my mother, a doctor, being at other people's service," Lacroix, who arrived in London from Wolfsburg in 2024, told L'Équipe. "So I have always had this idea that helping others was the most beautiful thing that you can do. I don't see it as a chore but rather an activity that makes me flourish.” He has also been in the business of making dreams come true. He recalls being stopped by an elderly man on the street who, after Palace's semi-final victory in the FA Cup last year, told him that he wanted to see Palace win a trophy before he died. Lacroix said that he thought of that man when Palace lifted the trophy at Wembley. Lacroix had only two caps by the time he was called up for the World Cup squad.
